rationale: The generated predicate det_get_my_end_point_equal is r1.id == r2.id where r1.id, r2.id are Vec<u8>. The hypothesis (r1@ == self_.my_end_point() && r2@ == self_.my_end_point()) only yields r1.id@ == r2.id@ (Seq<u8> view equality). In Verus, == on Vec<T> is strictly stronger than view equality and is opaque: there is no axiom relating Vec equality to Seq<u8> view equality. Confirmed via multiple isolated tests: v1@ == v2@ ⇒ v1 == v2 (fails), v1@ == v2@ ⇒ v1 =~= v2 (fails), v1@ == v2@ ⇒ v1 =~~= v2 (fails), v1@ == v2@ ⇒ equal(v1, v2) (fails), even with broadcast use of group_vec_axioms, group_laws_eq, lemma_vec_obeys_view_eq. get_my_end_point is external_body with ensures only ep@ == self.my_end_point(), so two return values can have identical views but different Vec instances. The orchestrator's predicate is over-strict (should be r1@ == r2@) and is genuinely undischargeable in Verus.
